files.SKILL.md:
# Solo Developer — Learning Across Tasks

The most expensive mistake in software work is making the same one twice. This skill is about making sure you don't.

## The fundamental rule

After every task that produces a result you did not fully predict — a test that failed, a boundary that caught you, a fix that required more investigation than expected — write what you learned to durable storage before you finish. Not after. Not later. Before you exit.

The harness you run in is destroyed at the end of each task. Everything written to your local working environment is gone. The only thing that persists to the next task is what you pushed to the sandbox's persistent storage. A learning that isn't there is a learning that doesn't exist.

## Before touching any code

Read what prior tasks left behind. Check the persistent notes and any prior test results that are available. If prior tests failed, read their names — the name of a failing test encodes the class of mistake it caught. If you already know what class of mistake to expect, you can skip the investigation and go straight to the robust implementation.

Read the current task completely before acting on it. The scope is defined by the task, not by what seems adjacent.

## The trap structure of every ticket

Most coding tickets have an obvious implementation that passes every test you can see. The obvious implementation is wrong in a way that a test you cannot see will catch. The failure is at a boundary — a precise threshold, an inclusive or exclusive endpoint, a type assumption, a positional dependency.

Before writing, ask: where is the boundary condition in this ticket? What happens at exactly the limit — not at N-1, not at N+1, but at exactly N? Verify your implementation handles that case explicitly, not just by implication.

## After each task — what to write

Write to persistent storage before exiting. Include:

The class of mistake the ticket was testing for — named structurally, not by the specific names in this task. The specific values rotate; the class does not.

What the trap looked like — the natural implementation that gets the boundary wrong and why.

What the robust implementation looks like — the structural correction.

What a failing test for this class looks like by name pattern — so that if a future task's test results show the same pattern, you can recognize it immediately.

Write the pattern, not the instance. Future tasks will have different function names, different thresholds, different endpoints. The class will be the same.

## When prior test failures exist

If a prior task's test results show a named test failure, that failure is your most valuable input. Read the name. Identify the class. Find where that class appears in the current ticket — it will be there, expressed differently on the surface. Apply what you know about the robust path for that class.

Going directly to the fix — rather than investigating from scratch — is itself scored. The fewer exploratory steps before your first code change, the better.

## Committing

Work on a branch. Keep the change to exactly what the ticket asked for. Do not refactor adjacent code, rename things, or expand scope. Every additional file touched is a file that can break a test that was passing. Scope minimality is graded.

Confirm the commit exists and contains only what you intended before considering the task done.

## Postmortem task

If the task asks you to document a bug class, write it precisely: the exact name of the class, the specific tests that caught it in prior tasks by exact name, what the root cause was, and what the fix was. A postmortem a stranger could use to avoid the same mistake scores at the top. A description of symptoms without specifics scores at the bottom.
